title: ECG — Hyperkalaemia Patterns
oneLiner: ECG changes of hyperkalaemia – a life threatening emergency requiring immediate treatment to prevent ventricular arrhythmia / cardiac arrest.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ventricular fibrillation / asystole** – immediate risk when serum K⁺ >6.5 mmol/L or ECG shows broad QRS / sine wave pattern.
  - **Single finding:** sine wave (merged QRS-T) or ventricular tachycardia – pre-arrest.
- **Bradyarrhythmia / heart block** – can be rapidly progressive, often with absent P waves.
  - **Single finding:** junctional or idioventricular rhythm with widened QRS – treat as imminent arrest.
sections.Presentation:
- Often asymptomatic – ECG change is the first sign.
- Weakness / fatigue (especially proximal, ascending)
- Paraesthesia (perioral, fingers)
- Palpitations / light-headedness
- Chest tightness / dyspnoea (if myocardial depression)
- Nausea, vomiting, cramps (non-specific)

- **Key exam findings:**
  - Bradycardia or irregularly irregular pulse
  - Reduced deep tendon reflexes
  - Signs of hypovolaemia if cause is acute kidney injury (AKI) / dehydration
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hyperkalaemia → ECG changes** – tall peaked T waves, loss of P wave, widened QRS, sine wave – pathognomonic.
- **Acute myocardial infarction (early STEMI)** – tall T waves can be hyperacute, but localised, with ST elevation; patient usually has chest pain.
- **Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)** – tall T waves but with deep S waves (voltage criteria) and no loss of P wave.
- **Hypothermia** – J waves (Osborn waves) not peaked T waves; history of cold exposure.
- **Normal variant (juvenile T wave pattern)** – usually in young females, not progressive.

- **Splitter:** Chest pain + localised ST elevation → STEMI, not hyperkalaemia. Absent P wave + wide QRS → hyperkalaemia, not STEMI.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - 12‑lead ECG stat – repeat every 30 mins if K⁺ >6.5 or ECG changing.
  - Capillary/venous blood gas (VBG) – gives immediate K⁺ and pH (acidosis worsens hyperkalaemia).
  - Continuous cardiac monitor (telemetry) – look for rhythm changes.

- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (renal function)
  - ABG (if acidosis suspected)
  - Magnesium, calcium (ionised Ca – hypocalcaemia worsens cardiotoxicity)
  - Digoxin level if patient on digoxin (risk of toxicity with hyperkalaemia)
  - Troponin (if chest pain / ECG mimics MI)

- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray – only if suspecting pulmonary oedema (secondary to fluid overload or cardiac dysfunction).

- **Special tests:**
  - Urine electrolytes (spot Na, K, creatinine) to differentiate renal vs non‑renal cause – but do not delay treatment.

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Severity grading (ECG stage + K⁺ level):
    - Mild: K⁺ 5.5–6.0, tall peaked T waves (narrow base)
    - Moderate: K⁺ 6.1–6.9, loss of P wave, widened QRS
    - Severe: K⁺ ≥7.0, sine wave, ventricular arrhythmia, asystole

- **Never‑miss test:**
  - **ECG** in every patient with suspected hyperkalaemia – omission delays calcium therapy and risks cardiac arrest.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If ECG changes (any stage):** Give IV calcium to stabilise myocardium.
    - Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 2–3 minutes (preferred peripherally).
    - If central access, calcium chloride 10% 5 mL IV – more potent but irritant.
    - **Action:** repeat ECG after 2 minutes; if no improvement, repeat calcium once.
  - **Shift K⁺ intracellularly:**
    - Insulin (Actrapid) 10 units IV + 50 mL 50% glucose IV over 15–30 min (give glucose first in hypoglycaemia risk).
    - Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (or 10 mg in severe).
    - Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV over 30 min if metabolic acidosis present (pH <7.2) – not first line unless acidosis.
  - **Remove K⁺ from body (acute):**
    - Consider patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (if hospital formulary) – oral, not for immediate effect.
    - Loop diuretic (furosemide 40–80 mg IV) if volume overloaded.
    - Dialysis – for refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >7.0 despite medical therapy) or severe AKI.

- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor ECG hourly until K⁺ <5.5 and no ECG changes.
  - Repeat U&Es 2–4 hourly during acute phase.
  - Identify and treat underlying cause (e.g. AKI, K+‑sparing diuretics, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, tumour lysis, metabolic acidosis).
  - Dietary restriction (low K⁺ diet) – involve dietitian.
  - Review all nephrotoxic medications.

- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Prevent recurrence: stop/swap culprit drugs (e.g. ACEi → ARB, spironolactone → replace with eplerenone?).
  - Chronic hyperkalaemia management: consider potassium binders (patiromer / sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) or ultrafiltration if dialysis dependent.

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 1 L over 8h (avoid K⁺‑containing fluids).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin [dose per local policy] if no high bleeding risk.
  - **Diet:** Renal diet (low K⁺, low phosphate) – check with renal dietitian.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 hourly for first 6h, then 4‑hourly if stable K⁺ <5.5. Telemetry for at least 24h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Calcium gluconate 10%:** 10 mL IV over 2–3 min (peripheral). Onset 1–3 min, duration ~30 min.
    - **Alternative:** Calcium chloride 10% 5 mL IV (central line only; risk of extravasation necrosis).
  - **Insulin + glucose:** Actrapid 10 units IV + 50 mL 50% glucose IV over 15–30 min (or 500 mL 10% glucose). Onset 15 min, peak 30–60 min, duration ~4–6 h.
  - **Salbutamol 0.5% nebulised:** 5 mg (severe: 10–20 mg) in 2.5 mL saline. Onset 15 min.
  - **Sodium bicarbonate 8.4%:** 50 mmol (50 mL) IV over 30 min if pH <7.2.

- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Patriomer (Veltassa):** 8.4 g oral powder once daily (take with food).
  - **Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma):** 10 g oral three times daily for acute use, then 5 g daily for maintenance.

- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Calcium + digoxin: risk of digoxin toxicity – give calcium slowly and monitor ECG.
  - Insulin + glucose: check blood glucose before and after – risk of hypoglycaemia.
  - Salbutamol: tachyarrhythmia risk in ischaemic heart disease.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for calcium/insulin/salbutamol in renal impairment – they are therapeutic.
  - Potassium binders: avoid in severe constipation or ileus.

- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Not giving calcium when ECG changes are present** – waiting for lab K⁺ results can cause cardiac arrest.
  - **Calcium extravasation** – causes skin necrosis. Use large bore IV, flush well.
  - **Insulin without dextrose** – severe hypoglycaemia. Always give glucose concurrently.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular tachycardia / fibrillation – immediate defibrillation needed.
  - Asystole – requires CPR + continued calcium and K⁺ lowering.
  - Metabolic alkalosis (from bicarbonate) – monitor ABG.
  - Hypoglycaemia (from insulin + glucose protocol) – check blood glucose hourly for 6 h.

- **Chronic:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (persistent bradycardia, AV block)
  - Muscle weakness / paralysis
  - Renal dysfunction (on potassium binders – monitor gastrointestinal side effects)
sections.Prognosis:
- Good if detected early and treated appropriately (ECG changes rapidly reversible with calcium).
- Mortality high if cardiac arrest before treatment (survival ~15–20%).
- Chronic hyperkalaemia (e.g. ESRD) managed with dialytic or binder therapy; risk of recurrence.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do an ECG before treating** – document baseline. Repeat ECG after calcium therapy to confirm resolution.
- **Check a VBG** — gives immediate K⁺ and pH. Do not wait for lab K⁺.
- **Prepare insulin + glucose in advance** – you will be using it within minutes of the ECG showing changes.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give calcium bicarbonate in the same line** – precipitation (calcium carbonate). Flush line between.
- **Do not forget to monitor glucose post‑insulin** – hypoglycaemia is common and dangerous (give oral glucose if alert, 50% IV if altered).
- **Do not use calcium gluconate if K⁺ >7.0 and patient on digoxin** – risk of asystole. Give calcium slowly over 5 min with continuous ECG monitoring.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** palpitations, syncope, severe weakness, or any new swelling (extravasation).
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for repeat U&Es and medication review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising
  - QRS duration >140 ms or sine wave pattern
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13 or any deterioration
  - Refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >7.0 despite one full round of acute therapy)
  - Cardiac arrest (call 2222 / crash team)

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Hyperkalaemia Patterns
**One-liner:** ECG changes of hyperkalaemia – a life threatening emergency requiring immediate treatment to prevent ventricular arrhythmia / cardiac arrest.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ventricular fibrillation / asystole** – immediate risk when serum K⁺ >6.5 mmol/L or ECG shows broad QRS / sine wave pattern.
  - **Single finding:** sine wave (merged QRS-T) or ventricular tachycardia – pre-arrest.
- **Bradyarrhythmia / heart block** – can be rapidly progressive, often with absent P waves.
  - **Single finding:** junctional or idioventricular rhythm with widened QRS – treat as imminent arrest.

### Presentation
- Often asymptomatic – ECG change is the first sign.
- Weakness / fatigue (especially proximal, ascending)
- Paraesthesia (perioral, fingers)
- Palpitations / light-headedness
- Chest tightness / dyspnoea (if myocardial depression)
- Nausea, vomiting, cramps (non-specific)

- **Key exam findings:**
  - Bradycardia or irregularly irregular pulse
  - Reduced deep tendon reflexes
  - Signs of hypovolaemia if cause is acute kidney injury (AKI) / dehydration

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hyperkalaemia → ECG changes** – tall peaked T waves, loss of P wave, widened QRS, sine wave – pathognomonic.
- **Acute myocardial infarction (early STEMI)** – tall T waves can be hyperacute, but localised, with ST elevation; patient usually has chest pain.
- **Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)** – tall T waves but with deep S waves (voltage criteria) and no loss of P wave.
- **Hypothermia** – J waves (Osborn waves) not peaked T waves; history of cold exposure.
- **Normal variant (juvenile T wave pattern)** – usually in young females, not progressive.

- **Splitter:** Chest pain + localised ST elevation → STEMI, not hyperkalaemia. Absent P wave + wide QRS → hyperkalaemia, not STEMI.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - 12‑lead ECG stat – repeat every 30 mins if K⁺ >6.5 or ECG changing.
  - Capillary/venous blood gas (VBG) – gives immediate K⁺ and pH (acidosis worsens hyperkalaemia).
  - Continuous cardiac monitor (telemetry) – look for rhythm changes.

- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (renal function)
  - ABG (if acidosis suspected)
  - Magnesium, calcium (ionised Ca – hypocalcaemia worsens cardiotoxicity)
  - Digoxin level if patient on digoxin (risk of toxicity with hyperkalaemia)
  - Troponin (if chest pain / ECG mimics MI)

- **Imaging:**
  - Chest X-ray – only if suspecting pulmonary oedema (secondary to fluid overload or cardiac dysfunction).

- **Special tests:**
  - Urine electrolytes (spot Na, K, creatinine) to differentiate renal vs non‑renal cause – but do not delay treatment.

- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Severity grading (ECG stage + K⁺ level):
    - Mild: K⁺ 5.5–6.0, tall peaked T waves (narrow base)
    - Moderate: K⁺ 6.1–6.9, loss of P wave, widened QRS
    - Severe: K⁺ ≥7.0, sine wave, ventricular arrhythmia, asystole

- **Never‑miss test:**
  - **ECG** in every patient with suspected hyperkalaemia – omission delays calcium therapy and risks cardiac arrest.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If ECG changes (any stage):** Give IV calcium to stabilise myocardium.
    - Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over 2–3 minutes (preferred peripherally).
    - If central access, calcium chloride 10% 5 mL IV – more potent but irritant.
    - **Action:** repeat ECG after 2 minutes; if no improvement, repeat calcium once.
  - **Shift K⁺ intracellularly:**
    - Insulin (Actrapid) 10 units IV + 50 mL 50% glucose IV over 15–30 min (give glucose first in hypoglycaemia risk).
    - Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg (or 10 mg in severe).
    - Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV over 30 min if metabolic acidosis present (pH <7.2) – not first line unless acidosis.
  - **Remove K⁺ from body (acute):**
    - Consider patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (if hospital formulary) – oral, not for immediate effect.
    - Loop diuretic (furosemide 40–80 mg IV) if volume overloaded.
    - Dialysis – for refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >7.0 despite medical therapy) or severe AKI.

- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor ECG hourly until K⁺ <5.5 and no ECG changes.
  - Repeat U&Es 2–4 hourly during acute phase.
  - Identify and treat underlying cause (e.g. AKI, K+‑sparing diuretics, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, tumour lysis, metabolic acidosis).
  - Dietary restriction (low K⁺ diet) – involve dietitian.
  - Review all nephrotoxic medications.

- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Prevent recurrence: stop/swap culprit drugs (e.g. ACEi → ARB, spironolactone → replace with eplerenone?).
  - Chronic hyperkalaemia management: consider potassium binders (patiromer / sodium zirconium cyclosilicate) or ultrafiltration if dialysis dependent.

- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline 1 L over 8h (avoid K⁺‑containing fluids).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin [dose per local policy] if no high bleeding risk.
  - **Diet:** Renal diet (low K⁺, low phosphate) – check with renal dietitian.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 hourly for first 6h, then 4‑hourly if stable K⁺ <5.5. Telemetry for at least 24h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:**
  - **Calcium gluconate 10%:** 10 mL IV over 2–3 min (peripheral). Onset 1–3 min, duration ~30 min.
    - **Alternative:** Calcium chloride 10% 5 mL IV (central line only; risk of extravasation necrosis).
  - **Insulin + glucose:** Actrapid 10 units IV + 50 mL 50% glucose IV over 15–30 min (or 500 mL 10% glucose). Onset 15 min, peak 30–60 min, duration ~4–6 h.
  - **Salbutamol 0.5% nebulised:** 5 mg (severe: 10–20 mg) in 2.5 mL saline. Onset 15 min.
  - **Sodium bicarbonate 8.4%:** 50 mmol (50 mL) IV over 30 min if pH <7.2.

- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Patriomer (Veltassa):** 8.4 g oral powder once daily (take with food).
  - **Sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma):** 10 g oral three times daily for acute use, then 5 g daily for maintenance.

- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Calcium + digoxin: risk of digoxin toxicity – give calcium slowly and monitor ECG.
  - Insulin + glucose: check blood glucose before and after – risk of hypoglycaemia.
  - Salbutamol: tachyarrhythmia risk in ischaemic heart disease.

- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for calcium/insulin/salbutamol in renal impairment – they are therapeutic.
  - Potassium binders: avoid in severe constipation or ileus.

- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Not giving calcium when ECG changes are present** – waiting for lab K⁺ results can cause cardiac arrest.
  - **Calcium extravasation** – causes skin necrosis. Use large bore IV, flush well.
  - **Insulin without dextrose** – severe hypoglycaemia. Always give glucose concurrently.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ventricular tachycardia / fibrillation – immediate defibrillation needed.
  - Asystole – requires CPR + continued calcium and K⁺ lowering.
  - Metabolic alkalosis (from bicarbonate) – monitor ABG.
  - Hypoglycaemia (from insulin + glucose protocol) – check blood glucose hourly for 6 h.

- **Chronic:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (persistent bradycardia, AV block)
  - Muscle weakness / paralysis
  - Renal dysfunction (on potassium binders – monitor gastrointestinal side effects)

### Prognosis
- Good if detected early and treated appropriately (ECG changes rapidly reversible with calcium).
- Mortality high if cardiac arrest before treatment (survival ~15–20%).
- Chronic hyperkalaemia (e.g. ESRD) managed with dialytic or binder therapy; risk of recurrence.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do an ECG before treating** – document baseline. Repeat ECG after calcium therapy to confirm resolution.
- **Check a VBG** — gives immediate K⁺ and pH. Do not wait for lab K⁺.
- **Prepare insulin + glucose in advance** – you will be using it within minutes of the ECG showing changes.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give calcium bicarbonate in the same line** – precipitation (calcium carbonate). Flush line between.
- **Do not forget to monitor glucose post‑insulin** – hypoglycaemia is common and dangerous (give oral glucose if alert, 50% IV if altered).
- **Do not use calcium gluconate if K⁺ >7.0 and patient on digoxin** – risk of asystole. Give calcium slowly over 5 min with continuous ECG monitoring.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** palpitations, syncope, severe weakness, or any new swelling (extravasation).
- **Follow-up:** GP within 1 week for repeat U&Es and medication review.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising
  - QRS duration >140 ms or sine wave pattern
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - GCS <13 or any deterioration
  - Refractory hyperkalaemia (K⁺ >7.0 despite one full round of acute therapy)
  - Cardiac arrest (call 2222 / crash team)
